visualGoal: Make a PC builder understand that Professional Ship Simulator's clickable cockpit means the hardware is optional and buy-last, then show the exact general-purpose gear worth adding. Real product proof throughout; the sim's own key art is the hero. No procedural art. Every embedded image is a verified marine-catalog photo.

realProductPhotosNeeded.product: Logitech G Saitek Pro Flight Throttle Quadrant (marine/engine-telegraph context)
realProductPhotosNeeded.notes: The single best-value engine-control pick, but the file path is NOT in verified-product-images.json, so it is text-only + Amazon link in the body. Needs a verified photo before it can appear as a shotgrid tile or gearpick, or before certification.

realProductPhotosNeeded.product: Logitech G29 Driving Force (as marine helm)
realProductPhotosNeeded.notes: Referenced as the optional helm stand-in. Path not in verified set, so text-only + Amazon link. Low priority because the article's thesis is that a wheel is optional here.

The subscription alternative that anchors the article's PSS-vs-NAUTIS-Home angle. No local image exists and it's not a catalog product; currently official-link only in the body. If we want a shotgrid/gearpick comparing the two sims, source and verify NAUTIS Home key art first (respect VSTEP asset licensing).

editorialCompositePrompts.prompt: Realistic home ship-sim bridge at dusk: a dual-monitor desk showing a teal radar/chart screen and a first-person clickable bridge cockpit, a general-purpose flight throttle quadrant and a button box arranged as an improvised engine console, a mouse resting front-and-center to signal the clickable cockpit, curator Gus Calder studying the input-binding menu, no fake branded hardware, no fake UI text, 16:9 editorial cover.
editorialCompositePrompts.negativePrompt: fake logos, fabricated boat wheel, warped levers, impossible ergonomics, tiny unreadable text, procedural gradient art, invented UI numbers

infographicPrompts.title: The three PSS rigs worth building
content: Rig 2 $120-220 throttle quadrant + button box (the sweet spot)
infographicPrompts.style: IgnitionSim command-center chart, large readable text, marine teal/brass palette, real product photos as row markers for Codex overlay

infographicPrompts.title: Professional Ship Simulator vs NAUTIS Home
content: PSS: one-time Steam purchase, Early Access 2026, clickable cockpit, modding roadmap
NAUTIS Home: subscription from ~EUR5.95/mo, 20+ ships, dual-screen radar/conning, course-backed
Both: run on repurposed PC gear, no dedicated boat helm
Choose PSS to buy once + mod; NAUTIS Home to play polished today
infographicPrompts.style: Two-column comparison card, marine palette, neutral (no star ratings), placeholders for both sims' key art

infographicPrompts.title: Read the input claims like an engineer
content: Reveal/Steam coverage: over 100 mappable buttons, several axes
Legacy Nautic XP site: over 50 buttons, 9 axes, triple-layer keyboard
Consistent both sources: fully clickable cockpit, joystick-to-gamepad per ship, NMEA/GPX
Verdict: exact count = check-current until EA input menu ships
infographicPrompts.style: Side-by-side claims panel, honest 'unsettled' framing, marine palette, no invented numbers

curatorScenePrompts.prompt: Realistic adult marine-sim curator Gus Calder at a home bridge desk, one hand on a flight throttle quadrant repurposed as an engine telegraph, calm methodical energy, an open controller-binding menu glowing on the monitor beside him, product overlay space to one side, practical not fantasy, 4:5 portrait.

screenshotsOrGameMediaNeeded.asset: Professional Ship Simulator clickable-cockpit / input-binding screenshot
screenshotsOrGameMediaNeeded.notes: Only use official Steam/Aerosoft press media with correct attribution; do NOT fabricate a UI. Useful to illustrate the clickable cockpit + per-ship controller setup once EA ships.
VSTEP-owned media; respect licensing. Would support the PSS-vs-NAUTIS-Home comparison visually.
